items.title: Open-weight AI just hit 2.8 trillion parameters…
items.excerpt: Last week, Chinese AI lab Moonshot dropped Kimi K3, a massive open-source monster that instantly parameter mogged every other open model in existence. And not just that, it has OpenAI and Anthropic terrified because its trust-me-bro benchmark performance is on par with, and in some cases beating, Claude Fable and GPT-5.6 Soul. And that's crazy because just a few weeks ago, the government was telling you these models were too dangerous for the common man to use. It only took the Chinese a few weeks to match their performance and then give away all the weights for free. But big AI is not too happy about this, and there are already calls to ban Chinese models completely in the United States. In today's video, we'll take a look at the massive breakthrough that is Kimi K3 and find out what it means for the future of artificial intelligence. It is July 22nd, [music] 2026, and you're watching The Code Report. Like Claude Fable and GPT-5.6 Soul, Kimi K3 is a native multi-model mixture of experts model with a 1 million token context window and a staggering 2.8 trillion parameters, and is optimized for jobs like long-horizon reasoning and, of course, coding. One interesting characteristic of K3 as a mixture of experts model is that it has 896 total experts, of which exactly 16 activate per token, which means it works just like a big corporation where 16 good programmers do all the work while 880 other managers sit there and do nothing. But apparently, this works out well for Kimi because it makes scaling about 2.5 times more efficient than K2. Despite these gains in efficiency, Kimi was so popular upon release that their GPUs ran out of juice and they had to start turning away paying customers. And as of making this video, all the paid plans are currently sold out. That's unfortunate, but in theory, you could self-host the model because the weights are open. The weights are expected to be released on July 27th, but there's no chance in hell you'll be able to run it on your little gaming GPU. To run a monster like this, you'll need a massive array of data center caliber GPUs. But in theory, if you could do that, you would have unlimited access to a Fable Soul caliber model

items.title: This $12 billion startup finally shipped something...
items.excerpt: Two years ago, Mira Murati did something we've all fantasized about. She woke up, decided she'd had enough of her boss, and quit her job as CTO of OpenAI with no backup plan and nothing but a vague desire to create time and space for her own exploration. If you or I did that, we'd probably end up coding HTML for food down on Market Street. But when someone like Mira does it, A16Z shows up with a $2 billion investment into any company she decides to build. The company was eventually Thinking Machines, and until last week, it's been known for being valued at $12 billion before having actually shipped anything. But that may have now just changed because they just released Inkling, a fully open weights model trained from scratch that can see, hear, reason, fine-tune, and potentially touch things in the real world. In today's video, we'll find out how it was built, what makes it interesting, and learn why Thinking Machines openly admits that it's not the best model in the world, and why that's the point. It is July 20th, 2026, and you're watching The Code Report. Last year, while the rest of us were spending time getting to know our newest Soham Parikh, Mira was quietly pulling off the greatest talent heist in Silicon Valley history. When she left OpenAI, she walked out with co-founder John Shulman, VP of research Barrett Zoph, and enough senior researchers to fill a Silicon Valley polycule. A year later, the dream team shipped Tinker, an API that lets you fine-tune open weight models without managing your own infrastructure or losing control over the training process. When it was released, ML researchers called it cool, which I imagine was a bit underwhelming if you'd just given them enough money to buy a Costco hot dog for one out of every six people on Earth. But that may have changed last week with the release of Inkling, a mixture of experts model with 970 billion total parameters. The trick is that it doesn't use all of them at once. Every token gets routed to a small subset of specialized experts, so only 41 billion parameters actually fire per token, giving you the most intelligence of a massive model at a fraction of the compute. And in this video, I'm going to show you the three meta patterns these survivors use to stay unfairly competitive. By the way, I'm Tim. Now, most products have at least one screen where the user reads numbers and have to make sense of it. And most apps stop at just showing the data. items.title: The Chart That Changed an Industry
items.excerpt: In 2014, ASIC released REP 413, its landmark review of retail life insurance advice. The report would become one of the most influential documents in the history of Australia’s life insurance industry. It triggered industry reviews. It influenced government policy. It shaped public debate. And ultimately it laid the foundations for the Life Insurance Framework (LIF). Nearly a decade later, I have a simple question. Has anyone asked whether it actually worked? How We Got Here To understand whether LIF worked, we first need to acknowledge that it was trying to solve a genuine problem. This wasn’t a case of regulators waking up one morning and deciding to reduce commissions. LIF was the culmination of almost a decade of reform aimed at restoring trust in financial advice. The FOFA Era The Future of Financial Advice (FOFA) reforms fundamentally changed Australia’s approach to financial advice. Following high-profile investment scandals, the focus shifted towards conflicted remuneration. The philosophy was simple: Advice should be driven by client interests, not adviser incentives. Investment commissions were largely removed because policymakers believed embedded remuneration structures created conflicts that distorted advice outcomes. Life insurance, however, was treated differently. There was widespread recognition that life insurance advice wasn’t like investment advice. There was no investment balance from which fees could be deducted. No growing pool of assets to fund advice. The concern was that banning commissions entirely would dramatically reduce access to insurance advice. Life insurance commissions survived FOFA. But only just. REP 413: The Spark In 2014, ASIC released REP 413, reviewing life insurance advice provided between 2006 and 2013. The findings were concerning. ASIC concluded that approximately 37% of advice files reviewed failed to comply with ASIC’s requirements. For a report that ultimately reshaped an entire industry, the evidentiary base was remarkably narrow. More importantly, ASIC observed that advisers receiving higher upfront commissions were more likely to provide advice that ASIC assessed as poor quality. This became the defining narrat

items.title: Nobody Asks the Loss Ratio of a Couch
items.excerpt: Before we go any further, let’s start with a simple question. What is a loss ratio? A loss ratio is simply the amount an insurer pays in claims divided by the premiums it receives. If an insurer collects $100 in premiums and pays $50 in claims, it has a 50% loss ratio. Sounds simple enough. The problem is that many people assume a higher loss ratio automatically means better value. I’m not convinced that’s true. If I told you a life insurance company only paid out around 50 cents in claims for every dollar of premium it received, would you consider that good value? My first reaction was that it sounded poor value. In fact, many people would probably think it sounds terrible. After all, where did the other 50 cents go? Let’s consider the two pillars of Life Insurance in Australia – Retail and Group. In retail advised life insurance, insurers will often target a loss ratio somewhere around 45-50%. In group insurance, via superannuation funds, loss ratios can often target 80%. At first glance, that makes group insurance look like better value. If one product has a loss ratio of 80% and another has a loss ratio of 50%, surely the 80% product is better value? If you’re expecting an actuary to answer this question with a simple yes or no, you’re going to be disappointed. One of our favourite phrases is: “It depends.” I’ve spent much of my career pricing life insurance products, and one thing I’ve learned is that a 50% loss ratio doesn’t necessarily tell you much about profitability, and an 80% loss ratio doesn’t necessarily tell you much about value. I’ve seen products with lower loss ratios generate less profit than products with much higher loss ratios. The economics are far more complicated than many people realise. Why an 80% loss ratio isn’t always better than 50% The difference isn’t because retail insurers are making a fortune. The economics are simply different. In retail insurance, a significant portion of the premium goes towards things that don’t exist in most group arrangements. Advice commissions. Underwriting. Medical evidence. items.title: Actuaries Institute Maps the Generational Flow of Australia's $1 trillion Tax and Spending System
items.excerpt: Actuaries Institute Maps the Generational Flow of Australia's $1 trillion Tax and Spending System Government spending follows a U-shaped pattern across Australians' lifetimes highest for children and those in their 80s while taxation peaks among working-age Australians. Two Australians with the same gross incomes of $100,000 one aged 30, one aged 71 end up with net incomes that differ by more than $42,000 once taxation, government spending and transfers are accounted for. The report identifies several policy considerations for the future, including age-based tax and spending settings that better consider means, broadening the tax base, overall fiscal discipline and supporting asset accumulation at younger ages. As Australia's population ages, questions of intergenerational equity and fiscal sustainability are becoming increasingly important. New Actuaries Institute analysis examines how Australia's $1 trillion tax and spending system is distributed across generations, how those patterns have changed over time, and what they may mean for the future. The report, Spotlight on the Australian Tax and Transfer System, is the first of two deep-dive papers expanding on the Australian Actuaries Intergenerational Equity Index (AAIEI) and contributes to the Led by Actuaries Institute members Dr Hugh Miller, Dr Laura Dixie and Shams Mehry, the report analyses government budget data, the National Transfer Accounts and the Household, Income and Labour Dynamics in Australia (HILDA) Survey to provide a detailed picture of how Australians contribute to, and benefit from, the tax and transfer system at different stages of life. The report found government spending follows a distinct U-shaped pattern across age groups with more spending on children and older Australians both per person and in aggregate, and less on working-age adults. Government spending averages approximately $41,000 per child each year, driven by education costs. Spending drops to around $23,000 for a typical 30-year-old. items.title: How Cinematographers Choose Their Grip Packages
items.excerpt: This video is sponsored by Squarespace: the all-in-one platform to build your website. From the largest swopping crane movements to the subtlest tracking shot, or even a shot mounted directly onto a vehicle, grips are responsible. Grip gear is the backbone of executing any safe and efficient camera motion or rigging a complex lighting setup. As a cinematographer who has worked on both small content shoots or indie productions, as well as industry sized feature film or series shoots, I’ll take you through a typical grips gear package often selected by DPs for both of these different scales of production. From head and legs, to dollies, gimbals and even grips gear for special occasions, let's get started. The one piece of gear that pretty much any production needs is a tripod. But not all tripods are the same. They come in different shapes and sizes and are mainly selected for the payload they can take. In other words, how big is the camera. For our indie shoot, which I’ve assumed will use something like a Sony FX6 or smaller, there are many different options but the tripod kit I’ve listed is the Cartoni Focus 12 with a payload of 12kg. This kit comes with a 100mm bowl tripod head which can be detached and legs that can adjust the tripod height or spread to get lower. To cover any low angles, where the camera needs to get very low to the ground, I’ve included a hi hat, a miniature tripod base which the 100mm tripod head can fit into. Because industry cinema cameras and lenses are larger and heavier, they need a tripod with a larger payload. The industry standard is the O’Connor 2575 fluid head designed for cameras up to 40.8kg - which supports basically any camera and heavy cinema zoom lens. This industry head is paired with heavy duty aluminium legs, which come in a short size, for a lower height, or tall legs for a higher angle shot. Unlike our indie tripod, which can be levelled by adjusting the round bowl, these heavy production tripods are bubbled by adjusting the individual height of each leg until the horizon is level.
